warfarin|aspirin.mechanism: PD: Additive — anticoagulation (warfarin) combined with antiplatelet effect and GI mucosal damage (aspirin)

ibuprofen|warfarin.mechanism: PD: Additive bleeding + PK: CYP2C9 inhibition by ibuprofen increases warfarin plasma levels

ibuprofen|lisinopril.mechanism: PD: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation, blunting ACE inhibitor effect and reducing renal perfusion

lisinopril|lithium.mechanism: PK: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone → increased sodium reabsorption → reduced lithium renal excretion → toxicity

amiodarone|digoxin.mechanism: PK: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein (P-gp), reducing renal and biliary digoxin excretion

ciprofloxacin|warfarin.mechanism: PK: CYP1A2 inhibition + reduction of vitamin K-producing gut flora

propranolol|salbutamol.mechanism: PD: Pharmacological antagonism — propranolol blocks β2-adrenoceptors, directly opposing salbutamol bronchodilation

levothyroxine|warfarin.mechanism: PD: Thyroid hormone increases catabolism of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, potentiating anticoagulation

ibuprofen|methotrexate.msg: FATAL Toxicity — NSAIDs block MTX excretion.
ibuprofen|methotrexate.mechanism: PK: NSAIDs inhibit OAT3 (organic anion transporter) in renal tubules, blocking methotrexate excretion → toxic accumulation

aspirin|methotrexate.msg: FATAL Toxicity — NSAIDs block MTX excretion.
aspirin|methotrexate.mechanism: PK: Aspirin inhibits renal tubular OAT3 transporter, blocking methotrexate excretion → toxic accumulation

ciprofloxacin|theophylline.mechanism: PK: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, the primary enzyme metabolising theophylline → 2-3x rise in theophylline levels

carbamazepine|erythromycin.mechanism: PK: Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4, the primary enzyme metabolising carbamazepine → elevated levels and toxicity

gentamicin|vancomycin.mechanism: PD: Additive — both drugs cause proximal tubular damage; combined use increases nephrotoxicity significantly

phenytoin|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Biphasic — phenytoin initially inhibits CYP2C9 (raises INR), then induces it long-term (lowers INR); highly unpredictable

apixaban|aspirin.msg: Increased bleeding risk; avoid unless benefit outweighs risk.
apixaban|aspirin.mechanism: PD: Additive — anticoagulation (apixaban) plus antiplatelet effect (aspirin) increases serious bleeding risk

apixaban|ibuprofen.msg: Severe bleeding risk — NSAIDs + DOAC combination.
apixaban|ibuprofen.mechanism: PD: Additive bleeding — NSAID impairs platelet function and causes GI mucosal injury on top of anticoagulation

apixaban|fluconazole.mechanism: PK: Fluconazole inhibits both CYP3A4 (metabolism) and P-gp (transport), significantly raising apixaban exposure

aspirin|enoxaparin.mechanism: PD: Additive — antiplatelet effect of aspirin combined with anticoagulant action of enoxaparin

enoxaparin|ibuprofen.msg: Increased bleeding risk — NSAIDs impair platelet function.
enoxaparin|ibuprofen.mechanism: PD: Additive — NSAID inhibits COX-1-mediated platelet aggregation on top of LMWH anticoagulation

aspirin|heparin.mechanism: PD: Additive — antiplatelet effect of aspirin potentiates anticoagulant action of heparin

fluconazole|warfarin.msg: Fluconazole markedly increases INR — major bleeding risk.
fluconazole|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Fluconazole is a potent CYP2C9 inhibitor — substantially reduces warfarin (S-enantiomer) metabolism

metronidazole|warfarin.msg: Metronidazole inhibits warfarin metabolism — INR rises sharply.
metronidazole|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, reducing warfarin metabolism; also inhibits gut flora vitamin K synthesis

rifampicin|warfarin.msg: Rifampicin is a potent CYP inducer — warfarin levels fall dramatically; INR drops.
rifampicin|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Rifampicin is a potent inducer of CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 — dramatically accelerates warfarin metabolism, causing sub-therapeutic anticoagulation

amiodarone|warfarin.msg: Amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism — risk of severe bleeding.
amiodarone|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Amiodarone (and active metabolite DEA) inhibit CYP2C9 — raises warfarin levels; effect persists weeks after amiodarone stopped

sodium valproate|warfarin.msg: Valproate displaces warfarin and inhibits its metabolism — increased bleeding risk.
sodium valproate|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Valproate displaces warfarin from plasma protein binding (albumin) and inhibits CYP2C9

celecoxib|warfarin.msg: Celecoxib may enhance anticoagulant effect of warfarin.
celecoxib|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Celecoxib inhibits CYP2C9, reducing S-warfarin metabolism → increased anticoagulant effect

amiodarone|bisoprolol.msg: Additive bradycardia and AV block risk.
amiodarone|bisoprolol.mechanism: PD: Additive — both drugs slow AV nodal conduction (amiodarone via K+ channel block; bisoprolol via β1 blockade)

amiodarone|metoprolol.msg: Increased risk of bradycardia and AV block.
amiodarone|metoprolol.mechanism: PD: Additive AV nodal depression — amiodarone (class III) + beta blocker (class II) combination

amiodarone|carvedilol.mechanism: PD: Additive — beta blockade (carvedilol) + AV nodal slowing (amiodarone) + additive vasodilation → hypotension

bisoprolol|verapamil.msg: Risk of asystole — never give together IV.
bisoprolol|verapamil.mechanism: PD: Additive — beta blockade (bisoprolol) + calcium channel block on AV node (verapamil) → complete AV block, asystole

metoprolol|salbutamol.mechanism: PD: Pharmacological antagonism — metoprolol blocks β2-receptors in bronchi, opposing salbutamol bronchodilation

atenolol|verapamil.msg: Risk of complete heart block and asystole.
atenolol|verapamil.mechanism: PD: Additive — atenolol blocks β1 (AV node), verapamil blocks L-type Ca2+ channels (AV node) → asystole risk, especially IV

amiodarone|ivabradine.mechanism: PD: Additive — both agents reduce heart rate (ivabradine via If current inhibition; amiodarone via multiple ion channels)

fluconazole|ivabradine.msg: Azoles increase ivabradine levels via CYP3A4 inhibition.
fluconazole|ivabradine.mechanism: PK: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4, the primary enzyme metabolising ivabradine → raised plasma levels → bradycardia

clarithromycin|digoxin.msg: P-gp inhibition raises digoxin to toxic levels.
clarithromycin|digoxin.mechanism: PK: Clarithromycin inhibits P-glycoprotein in gut and kidney, increasing digoxin absorption and reducing elimination

digoxin|furosemide.mechanism: PD: Indirect — furosemide causes hypokalemia; low K+ increases digoxin binding to Na+/K+-ATPase → toxicity at normal digoxin levels

lisinopril|spironolactone.mechanism: PD: Additive — ACE inhibitor reduces aldosterone (retains K+); spironolactone blocks aldosterone receptors (retains K+)

ramipril|spironolactone.mechanism: PD: Additive — ACE inhibitor + potassium-sparing diuretic combination; both reduce urinary K+ excretion

captopril|spironolactone.mechanism: PD: Additive — ACE inhibitor + potassium-sparing diuretic; combined use significantly increases hyperkalemia risk

lisinopril|potassium chloride.mechanism: PD: Additive — ACE inhibitor reduces aldosterone-driven K+ excretion; exogenous KCl further raises serum K+

ibuprofen|ramipril.msg: Reduced antihypertensive effect + acute kidney injury risk.
ibuprofen|ramipril.mechanism: PD: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, blunting ACE inhibitor vasodilation and reducing glomerular filtration

ibuprofen|losartan.msg: Reduced antihypertensive effect + renal impairment risk.
ibuprofen|losartan.mechanism: PD: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal perfusion, antagonising ARB antihypertensive effect

clindamycin|metronidazole.msg: Additive risk of C. difficile colitis — avoid prolonged combined use.
clindamycin|metronidazole.mechanism: PD: Additive microbiome disruption — both antibiotics severely disrupt colonic flora, increasing C. difficile overgrowth risk

linezolid|tramadol.msg: Risk of serotonin syndrome — potentially fatal.
linezolid|tramadol.mechanism: PD: Serotonergic synergism — linezolid is a weak MAO inhibitor (raises synaptic serotonin); tramadol inhibits serotonin reuptake

linezolid|sertraline.mechanism: PD: Serotonergic synergism — linezolid (MAO inhibition) + sertraline (SERT inhibition) → serotonin excess → potentially fatal

lithium|metronidazole.msg: Metronidazole may reduce lithium clearance — toxicity risk.
lithium|metronidazole.mechanism: PK: Metronidazole reduces renal lithium clearance by an uncertain mechanism; narrow therapeutic index makes this clinically significant

amiodarone|fluconazole.msg: Both prolong QT — risk of Torsades de Pointes.
amiodarone|fluconazole.mechanism: PD: Additive QT prolongation — amiodarone (IKr block) + fluconazole (hERG channel block) → Torsades de Pointes risk

fluconazole|simvastatin.msg: Fluconazole increases statin levels — myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk.
fluconazole|simvastatin.mechanism: PK: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4, the primary enzyme metabolising simvastatin → elevated statin levels → myopathy

fluconazole|ondansetron.msg: Both prolong QT — increased risk of arrhythmia.
fluconazole|ondansetron.mechanism: PD: Additive QT prolongation — both drugs block hERG (IKr) cardiac potassium channels

ciprofloxacin|ondansetron.mechanism: PD: Additive — ciprofloxacin and ondansetron both inhibit cardiac hERG potassium channels → QTc prolongation

amiodarone|azithromycin.msg: Both prolong QT — high risk of Torsades de Pointes.
amiodarone|azithromycin.mechanism: PD: Additive QT prolongation — azithromycin (hERG block) + amiodarone (IKr block) → high risk of Torsades de Pointes

azithromycin|warfarin.mechanism: PK: Azithromycin reduces gut flora responsible for vitamin K synthesis; possible CYP3A4 inhibition

metformin|rifampicin.mechanism: PK: Rifampicin induces OCT1 (organic cation transporter 1), increasing hepatic metformin uptake and reducing systemic bioavailability

dapagliflozin|rifampicin.msg: Rifampicin reduces dapagliflozin plasma levels via CYP induction.
dapagliflozin|rifampicin.mechanism: PK: Rifampicin induces UGT1A9 and CYP3A4, accelerating dapagliflozin metabolism → reduced glycemic efficacy

amlodipine|rifampicin.mechanism: PK: Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 — the primary amlodipine metabolising enzyme → amlodipine AUC reduced by ~90%

isoniazid|phenytoin.msg: Isoniazid inhibits phenytoin metabolism — risk of phenytoin toxicity.
isoniazid|phenytoin.mechanism: PK: Isoniazid inhibits CYP2C19, the primary enzyme for phenytoin hydroxylation → raised phenytoin levels → nystagmus, ataxia

carbamazepine|isoniazid.msg: Isoniazid inhibits carbamazepine metabolism — toxicity risk.
carbamazepine|isoniazid.mechanism: PK: Isoniazid inhibits CYP3A4, reducing carbamazepine clearance → toxicity; carbamazepine also induces isoniazid hepatotoxic metabolites

furosemide|gentamicin.mechanism: PD: Additive cochlear damage — both drugs are ototoxic; furosemide disrupts endocochlear potential, enhancing aminoglycoside uptake into hair cells

furosemide|lithium.msg: Furosemide reduces lithium excretion — toxicity risk.
furosemide|lithium.mechanism: PK: Furosemide-induced volume depletion stimulates proximal tubule sodium (and lithium) reabsorption → reduced lithium clearance

potassium chloride|spironolactone.mechanism: PD: Additive — spironolactone retains potassium by aldosterone blockade; exogenous KCl adds direct K+ load → severe hyperkalemia

carbamazepine|sodium valproate.msg: Complex interaction — may reduce valproate levels and increase carbamazepine toxicity.
carbamazepine|sodium valproate.mechanism: PK: Carbamazepine induces valproate metabolism (↓ valproate); valproate inhibits CBZ-epoxide hydrolase → accumulation of toxic CBZ-10,11-epoxide

aspirin|sodium valproate.msg: Aspirin displaces valproate from protein binding — increased free drug and toxicity.
aspirin|sodium valproate.mechanism: PK: Aspirin (at high doses) competes for albumin binding sites, displacing valproate → elevated free (active/toxic) valproate levels

methotrexate|sodium valproate.mechanism: PK: Valproate may reduce methotrexate renal clearance via OAT inhibition → elevated MTX levels and toxicity

amiodarone|carbamazepine.mechanism: PK: Carbamazepine is a potent CYP3A4 inducer — accelerates amiodarone (and DEA metabolite) metabolism → loss of antiarrhythmic efficacy

metformin|alcohol.mechanism: PD: Additive — alcohol inhibits hepatic lactate clearance; metformin reduces mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation → combined lactic acidosis risk

propranolol|regular insulin.msg: Propranolol masks hypoglycemia symptoms and prolongs hypoglycemic episodes.
propranolol|regular insulin.mechanism: PD: Propranolol blocks β2-adrenoceptor-mediated glycogenolysis and masks tachycardia (the main hypoglycemia warning sign)

metformin|regular insulin.msg: Additive blood glucose lowering; monitor for hypoglycemia.
metformin|regular insulin.mechanism: PD: Additive — insulin reduces blood glucose via cellular uptake; metformin reduces hepatic glucose output → combined hypoglycemic effect

dapagliflozin|furosemide.msg: Additive risk of dehydration and hypotension.
dapagliflozin|furosemide.mechanism: PD: Additive volume depletion — SGLT-2 inhibitor causes osmotic diuresis; loop diuretic causes natriuresis → combined dehydration/hypotension

sertraline|tramadol.mechanism: PD: Serotonergic synergism — sertraline (SERT inhibition) + tramadol (serotonin/noradrenaline reuptake inhibition) → serotonin excess

metoclopramide|tramadol.msg: Metoclopramide enhances tramadol absorption and effect.
metoclopramide|tramadol.mechanism: PK: Metoclopramide accelerates gastric emptying → faster tramadol absorption; also possible additive CNS effects

ketorolac|warfarin.msg: Significantly increased risk of serious GI bleeding.
ketorolac|warfarin.mechanism: PD: Additive — ketorolac inhibits platelet function and causes GI mucosal injury; warfarin anticoagulation amplifies bleeding severity

enoxaparin|ketorolac.msg: Simultaneous use contraindicated — high bleeding risk.
enoxaparin|ketorolac.mechanism: PD: Additive — NSAID (platelet inhibition + GI damage) combined with LMWH anticoagulation → high haemorrhage risk

heparin|ketorolac.msg: Simultaneous use contraindicated — high bleeding risk.
heparin|ketorolac.mechanism: PD: Additive — NSAID impairs haemostasis and causes GI injury while heparin anticoagulates → contraindicated combination

celecoxib|methotrexate.msg: COX-2 inhibitors reduce MTX excretion — toxicity risk.
celecoxib|methotrexate.mechanism: PK: Celecoxib inhibits renal OAT3 transporter, reducing methotrexate tubular secretion → toxic drug accumulation

isosorbide mononitrate|sildenafil.msg: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION — risk of fatal hypotension.
isosorbide mononitrate|sildenafil.mechanism: PD: Additive vasodilation — nitrates generate NO → cGMP; sildenafil inhibits PDE5 (cGMP breakdown) → catastrophic BP drop

isosorbide dinitrate|sildenafil.msg: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION — risk of fatal hypotension.
isosorbide dinitrate|sildenafil.mechanism: PD: Additive NO/cGMP-mediated vasodilation → profound, potentially fatal hypotension

nitroglycerin|sildenafil.msg: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION — risk of fatal hypotension.
nitroglycerin|sildenafil.mechanism: PD: Additive NO/cGMP-mediated vasodilation — nitrate + PDE5 inhibitor → catastrophic vasodilation and hypotension

hydralazine|metoprolol.msg: Additive blood pressure lowering — monitor for hypotension.
hydralazine|metoprolol.mechanism: PD: Additive antihypertensive effect — vasodilation (hydralazine) + reduced cardiac output (metoprolol beta blockade)

amiodarone|haloperidol.msg: Additive QT prolongation — risk of Torsades de Pointes.
amiodarone|haloperidol.mechanism: PD: Additive hERG/IKr blockade — both drugs are class IA/III QT-prolonging agents; combined use is high-risk

haloperidol|metoclopramide.msg: Additive risk of extrapyramidal effects and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
haloperidol|metoclopramide.mechanism: PD: Additive D2 receptor antagonism in basal ganglia and nigrostriatal pathway → EPS and NMS risk

diazepam|metronidazole.msg: Metronidazole inhibits diazepam metabolism — increased sedation.
diazepam|metronidazole.mechanism: PK: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C19, the primary enzyme hydroxylating diazepam → elevated levels → prolonged sedation

fluconazole|midazolam.msg: Fluconazole markedly increases midazolam levels — risk of profound sedation.
fluconazole|midazolam.mechanism: PK: Fluconazole strongly inhibits CYP3A4, almost completely blocking midazolam first-pass and systemic metabolism → severe over-sedation

clonazepam|sodium valproate.msg: May induce absence status — use with caution.
clonazepam|sodium valproate.mechanism: PD: Pharmacodynamic paradox — valproate raises CNS GABA; combination may precipitate absence status epilepticus in susceptible patients

methotrexate|tocilizumab.msg: Combined immunosuppression — risk of serious infection.
methotrexate|tocilizumab.mechanism: PD: Additive immunosuppression — IL-6 receptor blockade (tocilizumab) + antiproliferative effect (methotrexate) → increased infection risk

amiodarone|rosuvastatin.msg: Amiodarone inhibits rosuvastatin metabolism — myopathy risk.
amiodarone|rosuvastatin.mechanism: PK: Amiodarone inhibits BCRP and OATP1B1 transporters, reducing hepatic rosuvastatin uptake and increasing plasma levels → myopathy

calcium gluconate|digoxin.msg: IV calcium can precipitate digoxin toxicity and arrhythmia.
calcium gluconate|digoxin.mechanism: PD: Additive — both calcium and digoxin increase intracellular Ca2+ in cardiac cells; IV calcium raises extracellular Ca2+ → potentiates digoxin arrhythmogenicity

pregabalin|tramadol.mechanism: PD: Additive CNS depression — pregabalin (VGCC inhibition → reduced excitatory neurotransmission) + tramadol (opioid + monoaminergic) → sedation and respiratory risk
